title: Ash picture frame with mitre corners
subtitle: A simple four-piece frame with glued 45-degree mitre joints
excerpt: A picture frame in ash moulding with glued mitre corners. Each rail is cut to length with a 45-degree mitre at each end, glued at the corners, and clamped until set. An entry-level seasoned-wood project that builds the habit of accurate marking and consistent saw angles.
sourceNotes: Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery for mitre joint construction and clamping. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for frame-making and picture-frame construction.

glossaryTerms.definition: A joint in which each piece is cut at 45 degrees so that the two pieces meet at a right angle with no end grain showing on the front face.
The slot left by a saw blade. The kerf width must be accounted for when marking cut positions on frame rails.
Air-dried or kiln-dried wood at a stable moisture content, ready for precise joinery.

content.text: Cutting tools are sharp. Work with the blade travelling away from your body and your free hand behind the cutting edge at all times. Strop the tool before starting and whenever the edge feels rough.
A
mitre

joint picture frame has four moulded rails, each cut to the same 45-degree angle at both ends and glued to its two neighbours. The front face of the frame shows no end grain at the corners. The most common failure in mitre-joint frames is inaccurate angle: a 44-degree cut on one rail does not close against a 45-degree cut on the next.
Choosing the moulding
Use a simple flat or stepped moulding in
seasoned

ash, 30 to 40 mm wide and 15 to 20 mm thick. Avoid mouldings with a deep rebate at this stage; a simple profile is easier to cut cleanly and easier to clamp. The rebate for the glass and backing board can be added later with a router or by gluing a thin strip inside the frame.
Marking the lengths

Decide the inside dimensions of the frame (the opening that shows the picture). Each rail is marked so that the short face of the mitre corresponds to the inside dimension. Set the combination square to 45 degrees and mark the cut line with the marking knife. Account for the
kerf
: saw on the waste side of the knife line.
Sawing the mitres

Clamp each rail to the bench and saw along the knife line, keeping the saw at the correct 45-degree angle in both planes: across the face and through the thickness. Start the saw cut on the back arris, then lower the handle as the cut deepens. Check the angle with the combination square before cutting all four rails; if the first cut is not 45 degrees, adjust the saw hold before continuing.
Dry assembly

Assemble the four rails dry (without glue) and close the corners with the sash cramps. All four corners must close cleanly with no visible gaps before gluing. Small gaps mean one or more mitres are not at 45 degrees; trim the rail that is showing the open side of the gap.
Gluing and clamping

Apply PVA glue to both faces of each mitre. Assemble the frame and apply the sash cramps diagonally, pulling opposite corners together. Check the frame is square by measuring the diagonals: they must be equal. Adjust the cramp positions if one diagonal is longer. Leave under clamps for at least two hours.
Finishing

Sand lightly with 400-grit paper, working along the grain on each rail. Apply two coats of danish oil with a cloth, allowing the first coat to absorb fully before the second. Cut and fit a rebate strip inside the frame for the glass and backing, or have the frame glazed by a framer.
Variations
Splined mitre: reinforce the corner by sawing a slot across the glued mitre face and gluing in a thin cross-grain spline. Adds mechanical strength to the corner.
Painted finish: ash takes paint well. Prime the frame first; the tight grain needs no filler coat.

rows.symptom: Gap on the front face of one corner, none on the back
rows.cause: Saw cut is not square through the rail thickness
rows.fix: The front face gap means the outer face of the mitre is too short. Re-cut the rail, keeping the saw handle lower to maintain the angle through the full thickness.

rows.symptom: Frame is not square when the cramps are applied
rows.cause: One or more rails are cut to a slightly different inside length
rows.fix: Measure all four inside lengths before gluing. They must match in pairs (two long rails equal, two short rails equal). Re-cut the rail that is out.
